paragraphs: The divisions of the Roman territory.—Noted men of the period. CHAPTER XII. The design has been to make them as clear and open as possible; consequently, names and places not mentioned in the text have, as a rule, been omitted. North of the Padus were Verona, Mediolanum (Milan), Cremona, Mantua, Andes, and Vercellae, a noted battle-field.

The river Metaurus, noted for its defeat of Hasdrubal, was also located in Umbria. They likely came from the north and initially settled in the Po Valley; however, they were later displaced by the invading Gauls and moved farther south into Etruria. Among these cities, Volsinii served as the confederacy's capital, while Veii, Volaterrae, Caere, and Clusium were also notable.

The power of these people was gradually diminished by the Romans, and after the fall of Veii in 396, it became practically extinct. While the Hellénes were settling in Greece, the Italians entered Italy. A third tribe, named Luceres, composed possibly of conquered Latins, was later added and settled on the Coelian Hill.

All members of a gens were descended from a common ancestor, after whom the gens received its name. This body was probably originally composed of all the fathers of families, but in historical times it was limited to 300 members, holding life office and appointed during the regal period by the king. Consequently, during this later period, the number of senators greatly exceeded three hundred.

All Roman citizens belonged to a specific family, with a pater familias at its head. Each family was part of a particular gens, named after a common ancestor. Every gens was affiliated with a specific curia, and each curia was tied to a particular tribe. Alba was taken during the reign of Tullus Hostilius. His successor, Ancus Marcius, subdued several cities along the river and founded a colony at its mouth, which he named Ostia, Rome's seaport. It was not materially increased over the next two centuries.

In return, the latter gave them his support and looked after their interests. During Servius' reign, the demands of the plebeians, who had now become numerous, for more rights were met by the so-called Servian reform of the constitution. Each infantry soldier paid for his own equipment, while cavalrymen received a horse and food from the state to keep it.

The names of these kings are said to have been Lucius Tarquinius Priscus, Servius Tullius, and his son-in-law, Lucius Tarquinius Superbus. During this reign, a famous sewer known as Cloaca Maxima was also constructed to drain the Campagna. A temple in honor of Diana was erected on the Aventine during the reign of Tullius, serving all Latin towns.

Tarquinius Superbus added the territory of Gabii to the Roman Ager, planting two military colonies that were later lost. Around this time, the Senate, previously composed only of fathers of families (patres), welcomed some of the wealthiest landowners among the common people, and designated them as conscripti. The origin of the phrase "the Honorable" used by speakers addressing the Senate. "Parents and Children": These, however, could take no part in debates, nor could they hold magistracies. The wars in which the Romans were engaged during the preceding century before the establishment of the Republic had impoverished the state and crippled its commerce.

This burden was felt by all classes, but especially by the small landed plebeians whose fields had been devastated. As a result, the cavalry service at this time consisted entirely of young patricians, while the older ones were relegated to the reserve corps. This meant that the brunt of military duty fell on the plebeians. Upon their return from battle, however, those who had been set free were once again imprisoned.

After a well-deserved victory, the soldiers returned to the city walls. However, instead of celebrating, they abandoned their posts and marched to a nearby hill, where they took control, demanding that their grievances be addressed or else they would establish a new city. The First Secession of the Plebs occurred in 494 BCE. Those in debt were released from their obligations. The plebeians received the right to choose annually, from among themselves, two officers called Tribunes of the Plebs. These officials were responsible for protecting their interests and had the power to veto any action taken by a magistrate in the city.

This power, however, was confined within the city walls, and could never be exercised outside of them. The person of the Tribunes was also made sacred, to prevent interference with their duties while in discharge. If anyone attempted to stop them, he was committing a capital crime. Later, the number of Tribunes was increased to five, then ten.

The state had the right to claim this land at any time. However, the magistrates chose not to collect dues from the occupants. Valerius Publius, a Tribune, now proposed and carried, despite violent opposition from the patricians, a measure whereby the Tribunes would henceforth be elected by the Comitia Tributa rather than the Comitia Centuriata. The Roman Republic adopted the Terentilian Rogation, a proposal made by Gaius Terentilius Harsa, a Tribune, in 461 BC. He suggested that the laws from then on should be written down.

A compromise was reached, and it was decided to suspend the office of Consul for a time, with six Military Tribunes elected annually in the Comitia Centuriáta. The office was open to all citizens, but patricians dominated the selection process for a long period, preventing any plebeian from being elected. Over time, this office became the most sought-after position at Rome.

The patricians, despite obstinate resistance, suffered loss after loss. In contrast, the plebeians continued until all offices, except that of Interrex, were open to them. Following 200, plebeians could also fill the sacred offices of PONTIFEX and AUGUR.

The Roman patricians still held aloof from the commons, but their rights as citizens were no greater than those of the plebeians. The Celts delayed three days before marching on Rome. During this siege, the records of the city's history were destroyed, and we have no trustworthy data for events prior to 390.

The inhabitants of Rome were known as CIVES SINE SUFFRAGIO, or "citizens without suffrage." Over the next decade (353-343 BCE), Rome conquered the lowland regions as far south as Tarracina. Following a treaty in 354 BCE, both the Romans and Samnites waged war against the Volsci independently of each other. The first colony established was Antium in 338 BCE, followed by Tarracina in 329 BCE, Minturnae, and Suenessu in 296 BCE.

Rome's power was more compact and concentrated, while the Samnites excelled in numbers but were more dispersed. During the first five years of the war (326-321), Rome enjoyed greater success, forcing the Samnites to seek peace. However, after a bloody but ultimately unsuccessful attempt to drive them back, Rome itself was compelled to yield. Meanwhile, Pontius had occupied the defile behind their lines, leaving them with no choice but to surrender.

The Etruscans made their final stand against Roman power. The consul vowed his life to the infernal gods if victory were won, and with a fierce determination, he rushed into the heart of the enemy lines, where he fell. It is said that the father of Decius acted in a similar manner during a battle of the Latin War. His soldiers, inspired by his example, rallied and pushed back the Gauls. In ancient Rome's early days, when Greece was still relatively unknown, it was customary for them to establish colonies elsewhere to alleviate the strain caused by a rapidly growing population.
Tarentum had sent envoys to ask for aid from Pyrrhus, the young and ambitious King of Epirus. The Romans responded promptly, challenging him to battle. Nevertheless, they retreated in good order.

Pyrrhus was deeply moved by the bravery of his enemy and declared, "If we win another battle like this one, I will retreat with nothing left but empty ships." He recognized the inferior qualities of his Greek allies and decided to make a peace. He was noted for his eloquence, which was said to have gained more for his master than a sword. He therefore retired into winter quarters at Tarentum, where he received the famous embassy of Gaius Fabricius, sent to propose an exchange of prisoners.

The Ager Románus was subdivided into 33 districts (tribes) for voting and financial purposes, with four located in Rome. The Latin military colonies, now numbering 22, were later increased to 35.

a. Three notable men of the period, discussed in the previous chapters, included Appius Claudius, a censor and patrician, as well as Manius Curius Dentátus and Gaius Fabricius, who were plebeians.

All plebeians who owned land belonged to one of the tribes and could vote in the Comitia Tribúta. However, this excluded those who owned no land, including the plebeians of the city and freedmen, who were often educated and professional men such as doctors and teachers. Appius Claudius served as Censor in 312. He removed the exclusive right of landowners to vote in the Comitia Tributa, granting the right to vote to all property owners. The Appian aqueduct brought Rome its first regular water supply during the censorship of Appius. The road was later extended to Brundisium, passing through Venusia and Tarentum.

A man of sterling qualities, frugal and unassuming, after his public life, he retired to his farm and lived out the rest of his days in quiet solitude. The Carthaginians were said to have originated from Phoenicia, located on the eastern coast of the Mediterranean. The Latin student is, of course, familiar with Virgil's story of Dido and Aeneas. While Rome was expanding its territory from Latium to the Strait of Messina, on the opposite shore of the Mediterranean, opposite Italy and less than 100 miles from Sicily, a new power emerged: the Carthaginian power.

Rome had made two treaties with Carthage. The first was established shortly after the founding of the Republic, around 500 BC, while the second treaty was concluded approximately 340 years later. Although Rome was allied with Hiero, she decided to aid the mercenaries who had been executed in Rhegium for their role in the events that mirrored the actions of the Mamertines in Sicily. The Carthaginians intervened, forming a truce between Hiero and the brigands. The siege of Messána was lifted.

While on its way, it gained a victory over the Carthaginian fleet off the Herméan promontory, sinking 114 enemy ships. A new Carthaginian commander, Hamilcar Barca (Lightning), took the field in Sicily. The Carthaginians were engaged during this time in crushing a mutiny of their mercenary troops.

The plans of the invaders failed, and they were trapped between two Roman armies near Telamon in 222, and annihilated. The Via Flaminia, a major northern road, was extended from Spolegium to Ariminum. The Comitia Centuriáta was reorganized on the basis of tribes. Carthage was not idle. After subduing the revolt of the mercenaries in 1544, she formed the project of obtaining Spain as compensation for the loss of Sicily, Sardinia, and Corsica. Hamilcar Barca established a firm foothold in southern Spain through energetic measures.

After the assassination of Hasdrubal in 220, Hannibal, son of Hamilcar, emerged as the most capable leader. Despite being only twenty-eight years old, he had accumulated a wealth of experience. He therefore laid siege to Saguntum, a Spanish town allied with Rome.

Hannibal hurried up the valley of the Rhone, crossing the Isara, through the fertile country of the Allobroges. After sixteen days from Avenio, he arrived at the pass of the first Alpine range (Mont du Chat). The next day, after a long and tiring effort, the army reached the summit of the pass. A cavalry skirmish ensued, in which Hannibal was wounded; his son, a young man of seventeen, later known as Africanus, rescued him.

Meanwhile Sempronius, who had been recalled from Sicily, relieved the disabled Scipio. While the Carthaginian was wintering near Placentia, the Romans stationed troops to guard the two highways leading north from Rome and ending at Arretium and Ariminum. The Consuls for this year were Gaius Flaminius and Gnaeus Servilius. Hannibal, however, had the start, and was now near Lake Trasimene. He did not march on Rome but instead turned through Umbria, devastating the country as he went.

After giving his army a rest, he proceeded along the coast into Southern Italy. Meanwhile, Hannibal again crossed the mountains into the heart of Italy, reaching Beneventum and then Capua, the largest Italian city under Roman control. The Dictator followed, condemning his soldiers to a state of inaction while the enemy's cavalry plundered their allies.

Hannibal ordered his light-armed troops to ascend the heights at night, driving before them oxen with burning faggots tied to their horns. This gave the appearance of an army marching by torchlight. Hannibal's superiority as a general is unquestionable. His lack of success after this was due to insufficient support from home, and the fact that Rome's resources were greater than Carthage's.

It was an easy matter to conquer the enemy in open battle, but supporting his own army proved more difficult, as all of Italy had been devastated. Hannibal realized that more active measures than those already employed were necessary. In 212, while the Carthaginians besieged Tarentum in the extreme south of Italy, the Romans made strenuous efforts to recover Campania and Capua.

The city and its territory were declared to be Roman territory, and it was subsequently repopulated by Roman occupants. During his long reign of more than fifty years, he had been the steadfast friend and ally of Rome in her struggles with Carthage. The young tyrant, who was arrogant and cruel, was assassinated after reigning for a few months.

A two-year siege (214-213) followed, notable for the innovative devices developed by the renowned mathematician Archimedes. Shortly after, Scipio fought Hasdrubal, the brother of Hannibal, at Baeculae in the upper valley of the Baetis (Guadalquivir). However, the battle was not decisive, as Hasdrubal soon crossed the Pyrenees with a considerable force on his way to Italy.

Upon returning, he put down a soldier revolt sparked by discontent over pay. He then relinquished command and headed to Rome, with the intention of becoming a contender for the consulship. The task of checking Hannibal in Bruttium was entrusted to one person, while the duty of intercepting Hasdrubal was given to another. The war in Italy was now virtually ended. For although Hannibal had stood at bay in a corner of Bruttium for four years, he was powerless to prevent the restoration of Roman authority throughout Italy.

The old-fashioned aristocracy, however, did not like him, as his taste for splendid living and Greek culture was particularly offensive to them. A party in the Senate would have recalled him, but the popular enthusiasm in his favor was too strong to be resisted. Scipio twice defeated Syphax and the Carthaginian forces in 203. After all the newly enlisted troops of Hannibal had been killed or put to flight, his veteran soldiers, who had remained loyal to him in Italy, despite being vastly outnumbered on all sides by enemy forces, continued to fight on and were eventually killed one by one alongside their beloved commander.

Under incompetent rulers, she saw portion after portion of her dominions fall from her. From this country had come in part her supply of corn during the Second Punic War. In 200, Consul Publius Sulpicius Galba was sent across the Adriatic with a considerable force. His campaign and that of Consul Villius the following year yielded no decisive results. However, in 198, Consul Titus Quinctius Flamininus, a man of superior caliber, conducted the war with vigor.

With the first he broke through the line of the legions, which, however, closed in around him with little loss. The other division was attacked by the Romans while it was forming and thoroughly discomfited. The chief result of the second Macedonian war was therefore the firm establishment of a Roman protectorate over Greece and Egypt.

However, he claimed for himself portions of Asia Minor and Thrace, which Philip had previously held, and which Rome now declared free and independent. He crossed the Hellespont into Thrace in 196 but did not dare to enter Greece, despite being urged by the Aetolians, until after Flamininus had withdrawn his troops (192). This great soldier, after his defeat at Zama, continued to pursue his life's aim.

He fled and, after wandering from coast to coast, became the trusted adviser of the Syrian king. If Antiochus had been energetic after his arrival in Greece, he might have achieved something before the Roman troops arrived. Meanwhile, at Rome, the political enemies of the Scipios were gaining power.

Asiaticus was accused of misappropriating funds obtained during his campaign in the East. Asiaticus, however, was sentenced to pay a fine. When it was afterwards intimated that his brother too was implicated, he proudly reminded his enemies that their insinuations were ill-timed, for it was the anniversary of Zama.

His wife, Aemilia, was the daughter of Paullus, who fell at Cannae, and the sister of him who later conquered Perseus of Macedonia. His daughter, Cornelia, afterwards became the mother of the famous Gracchi. During a campaign in the East, he met Hannibal at the court of Antiochus.

Hannibal reportedly stated that Alexander was his greatest general, followed by Pyrrhus. If he had defeated Scipio, he would have ranked himself above both. Hannibal, following his defeat at Antioch, was forced to flee by the Romans. He sought refuge in Crete, where he later allied himself with Prusias, the king of Bithynia. The conquest of Macedonia and Greece was a pivotal event in ancient history. In 334 BCE, Alexander the Great, king of Macedon, began his campaign against the Persian Empire. He defeated the Persians at the Battle of Granicus in 334 BCE and then turned his attention to the Greeks. Alexander's army marched into Asia Minor, conquering the cities of Ionia and Phrygia along the way. In 333 BCE, he defeated the Persians again at the Battle of Issus, which led to the capture of Tyre and Gaza. In 332 BCE, Alexander invaded Greece proper, starting with the city of Thessaly. He then marched on to Athens, where he was met with resistance from the Athenian general, Epaminondas. However, Alexander's superior military tactics and the support of his loyal companions eventually led to the capture of Athens. The final stage of Alexander's conquest of Greece took place in 331 BCE, when he defeated the Thebans at the Battle of Chaeronea. With this victory, Alexander became the undisputed ruler of Greece, and his empire would go on to stretch from Greece to India. (171-146 Although Philip had aided the Romans in their campaign against Antiochus, he did not receive the expected reward.

He was sent to Ulterior Spain as governor in 191, where he put down all insurrections after some reverses. Shortly afterwards, he was captured, but was treated with kindness by the Consul. Perseus spent his last days in confinement near Rome, enduring base and cruel treatment.

After the victory at Pydna, the sympathy shown in Greece for the conquered monarch made the Romans more watchful of her interests there. Trouble again arose in Macedonia twenty years after Pydna, culminating in what is sometimes called the Fourth Macedonian War (149-146). Finally, when some sympathizers with Masinissa were banished from the city, he attacked and defeated the Carthaginians, compelled their army to pass under his yoke, and afterwards destroyed it (150).

After this victory, matters came to a crisis. When the ambassadors appeared before the Consuls again, they were told that Carthage must deliver all her arms and artillery; for, they said, as Rome was able to protect her, there was no need for Carthage to possess arms. The preservation of peace required that the people should quit the city, give up their navy, and build a new town without walls at a distance of ten miles from the sea.

Two years after the siege began, Publius Cornelius Scipio Africanus, the Younger, was elected consul at the age of thirty-seven, despite being under the legal minimum, in order to take charge of the siege. After two years of intense fighting and remarkable bravery by the defenders, the starving garrison could hold out no longer. Following the Battle of Zama, most of Syphax's possessions fell to Masinissa, including Sophonisba, whom he later married.

Scipio, fearing her influence over him, demanded she be taken as a Roman captive. She took poison. After nine years of provincial government (206-197), the country was divided into two provinces, separated by the Iberus River. Each province was assigned to a praetor. It wasn't until some time later that Spain was brought into a state of complete peace and order.

During seven years (147-140), he defeated the armies sent against him repeatedly. The only check to these successes came during the command of Metellus Macedonicus (143); upon his recall, matters returned to their former state. However, the Celtibéri remained in arms.

After devoting several months to disciplining his troops, he began a regular siege of the place. The civilizing process, due to Roman influence, progressed rapidly in these parts, while the interior remained in a state of barbarism. During the Numantine war, a conflict with the slaves erupted in Sicily, where they had been subjected to particularly harsh treatment.
Tiberius (14-37 AD) joined the army and served under his brother-in-law during the Third Punic War.

Africánus, upon hearing of his brother-in-law's murder, exclaimed, "Justly slain." The agrarian law, however, which had been passed, was too evident to be openly ignored. For a time after his brother's death, he retired from politics and served in the army in Africa and Sardinia, where he held the position of Quaestor. Nevertheless, the results obtained by the Gracchi still remained.

After the death of Alexander, the city became one of the most prosperous and famous in Asia Minor, known for its architectural monuments, a fine library, and renowned schools. Following the destruction of Carthage, Numidia emerged as the most significant kingdom in Africa.

After Micipsa's death, Jugurtha killed one of his cousins, Hiempsal, and seized the entire kingdom. He then attacked another cousin, Adherbal, who sought help from Rome. Adherbal was later captured, brutally tortured, and ultimately killed. The Senate ordered him to leave. As he departed, he exclaimed, "Venal city, destined for destruction, if a buyer is found." War had now begun in earnest, but resulted in a crushing defeat of the Romans, whose army was sent under the yoke.

He went, however, and was elected in 107, being the first plebeian chosen for that office in over a century. The western portion of Numidia was given to Bocchus as a reward for his treachery, while the remainder was governed by native princes until the civil war between Caesar and Pompey.

The barbarians, on their return from Spain, separated their forces. The Cimbri marched around the northern foot of the Alps towards Noricum, intending to invade Italy from that quarter. Meanwhile, the Teutones remained in Gaul. The Cimbri had crossed the Alps and were ravaging the fertile fields of Lombardy, meeting with little opposition from Catulus, one of the Consuls.

He died shortly after, and it was suspected that he was a victim of treachery. When vacancies occurred due to death or removal, they were filled by the Censor. The Censor appointed a person who had held one of the following offices: Dictator, Consul, Praetor, Curule Aedile, or, after the time of Sulla, Quaestor. They were named for their initial service as horsemen in the army, but over time, the cavalry consisted solely of allied troops.

It is not strange, therefore, that opinions at Rome began to shift towards a more liberal policy. Although seemingly indolent, he was naturally a soldier, statesman, and diplomat. After this campaign, Sulla lived in Rome for several years, taking no part in politics, and during this time his name and that of his rival were almost forgotten.
Sulla marched into the city at the head of his troops after 80,000 persons had perished. Fortunately, Marius died just 16 days after taking office, bringing an end to the bloodshed.

After a long siege, he captured and pillaged the city on March 1, 86. Meanwhile, Sulla's lieutenant Lucullus raised a fleet and won two victories off the coast of Asia Minor. Eventually, Sulla emerged victorious under the walls of Rome.

The Consuls and Praetors were to remain at Rome for their first year of office, then proceed to the provinces as Proconsuls and Propraetors. Upon Sulla's death in 78, CRASSUS and LEPIDUS were elected Consuls; however, the tumultuous nature of the time led them to swear an oath not to raise an army during their consulship. Crassus was defeated by Pompey in 78, and he died shortly thereafter.

He was a supporter of Marius during his troubles with Sulla, and upon Sulla's return from the East, he left Rome for Spain, where he took the lead of the Marian faction. The goal of Sertorius was to establish a government in Spain modeled after the Roman system. Meanwhile, a dangerous enemy was threatening Italy within its own borders.

Mithradates meanwhile took advantage of the troubles at Rome and again went to war. In 74, Lucius Licinius Lucullus was sent against him. After reorganizing and disciplining his army, Lucullus joined forces with his colleague and drove Mithradates into Pontus. He then defeated Mithradates at Cabira in 72 and his fleet at Tenedos in 71, forcing the king to seek refuge with his son-in-law, Tigranes, King of Armenia. Sulla later honored him with the title Magnus, which he retained ever after.

Pompey's ruling passion was avarice, although he distributed large sums to the people for political gain. After the expiration of his consulship, Pompey remained in Rome for three years (67-70), during which time the civil wars escalated, prompting the city to take an active role against them.

His decision was contrary to the wishes of the people, and he led his army against Jerusalem, which he captured after a three-month siege. Meanwhile, Mithradates returned to Pontus for the prosecution of his old design; however, the terror inspired by Roman arms was so great that even his own son refused to join him. Desperate at the turn of affairs, the aged monarch ended his life in 63, after a reign of fifty-seven years.

Pompey returned to Rome after a seven-year absence on January 1, 61, and was greeted with a well-deserved triumph. He was 45 years old. He had accomplished a truly remarkable achievement, founding several cities that later became centers of Greek culture and learning. He was celebrated for his conquests in Spain, Africa, and Asia. Caesar was first mentioned during Cinna's consulship in 86 BC. He was described as a young man of exceptional physical appearance, characterized by his height, slender build, and striking facial features. His eyes were dark and piercing, while his skin had a sallow complexion.

Shortly after the death of his father, in 84, Caesar married Cornelia, the daughter of Cinna. Influential friends intervened on his behalf, and the Dictator was finally persuaded to pardon him; however, he did so with reluctance, remarking that Caesar would go on to make many a notable leader like Marius. Upon learning of Sulla's death, Caesar immediately left the army and returned home (77).

While on his way, Caesar was captured by pirates and not released until a ransom of $50,000 was raised and paid. Meanwhile, Cicero, a companion from his youth, began to attract attention at Rome. He participated in the Social War (89), but during the tumultuous period that followed, he remained quietly engaged in literary pursuits.

Shortly after his return, Verres was elected Quaestor in 75 B.C., and thus became a member of the Senate. As a follower of Sulla, he had amassed some property during the proscriptions. Later, he held official positions in Greece and Asia, where he gained notoriety for his greed and cruelty.

Verres collected property worth $4 million. The former, although only six years younger, was already a great man and a distinguished soldier. While Pompey was absent in the east, matters at Rome were growing increasingly dire, and were taking shape for a swift collapse of the republic. Many people had suffered under Sulla and longed to recover what they had lost. Others, however, had been enriched by his dictatorship but squandered their ill-gotten gains, now waiting for a new leader to launch another attack on the state.

The people, however, believed in Caesar. Meanwhile, Pompey had returned from the East. Despite Bibulus' interference, Cato spoke out, and the Tribunes vetoed, yet the Assembly still passed the law, and additionally voted that the Senate be required to take an oath to observe it.

The Julian Laws were a code of laws that Caesar drew up during his term of office. He had to ensure the city was left in safe hands, or else all his efforts would be undone. In Rome, there were only two individuals who posed a significant threat if Caesar were to leave them behind.

The Senate had delayed accepting the bequest, and in the meantime, the island was ruled by Ptolemy of Cyprus, one of the heirs of the deceased king. His early service in Asia and his more recent campaigns in Spain had demonstrated his aptitude for military life. As a result, they proposed to emigrate further into Gaul, and the Sequanians, whose lands bordered on those of the Helvetians, granted them permission to march through their country.

The Helvetians were met at Bibracte, near Autun, and after a terrible battle that raged from noon until night, they were defeated with great slaughter. After several fruitless attempts at negotiation, during which the bad faith of Ariovistus became apparent, the forces came together. While Caesar was in Hither Gaul, he learned from Labiénus that the Belgae were forming a league to resist the Romans.

One day, while the six legions formed camp on the river Sabis' bank, the Nervii and their allies suddenly attacked from an ambush in the woods on the opposite bank. Meanwhile, another wave of Germans crossed the Rhine into Gaul during that winter. In 55-54 BC, Caesar ordered the construction of a large fleet, as he planned to return to Britain the following year.

After all preparations were completed, he set sail for July 20, 54, and the next day landed on the island. The following summer, Caesar again crossed the Rhine. He proposed to cut off Caesar's supplies by burning all the towns of the Bituriges and laying the country waste.

Caesar marched towards Thither, but after a well-defended siege, he captured the town and killed every person in it, excepting eight hundred who escaped to the camp of Vercingetorix. Vercingetorix was taken captive and displayed in the triumph at Rome, where he later died in a dungeon. During the nine years (59-50) Caesar spent in Gaul, great confusion prevailed at Rome.

Caesar met with opposing parties in bloody conflicts day by day. During the interval between his two campaigns, one in 57 and the other in 56, Caesar strengthened his alliance with his colleagues through interviews held at Ravenna and Luca. He retained command of Gaul, while Pompey controlled Spain and Crassus led Syria. The Parthians eventually surrendered, but only to lure him into an ambush, where he ultimately met his demise after performing great acts of bravery.

But he requested permission to set aside this custom and become a candidate while in command of the army in the province. Their first step was to weaken Caesar and provide their champion, Pompey, with a force in Italy. They voted that Caesar should return to Pompey a legion loaned to him, and also send another legion back to Italy. Meanwhile, news of his movements reached Rome.
Pompey, however, remained at the location, with 12,000 troops, awaiting transport to take them away. After three days of fruitless debate, Caesar chose to act on his own.

He had not succeeded in gathering a naval force in the Adriatic, and he had lost his promising lieutenant, Curio, who had been killed in Africa while pursuing Cato and Pompey's followers. During Caesar's absence, affairs at Rome returned to normal. Pompey, after nine months of gathering his forces in Epírus, was preparing for action.

He set sail from Brundisium on January 4th and arrived at Acroceraunia after a voyage. He was therefore compelled to confine himself to trivial operations, until his lieutenant, Mark Antony, could fit out a second fleet and bring over the rest of his legions. The hill of Pharsalus. The Battle of Pharsalus (September 9, 48 BCE) holds a significant place in history as it was a decisive conflict between the Roman aristocracy and Julius Caesar's forces, with the outcome determining Rome's future.

Two hundred of Caesar's men fell, while 15,000 of the enemy lay dead on the field. The wealthy elite constructed homes made of turf, adorned with ivy that cascaded over the doorways to shield their sensitive skin from the sun's rays. Plush couches were arranged for them to recline after their anticipated triumph, while tables were set with fine food and wine for a lavish feast. He was indeed a skilled soldier who excelled in various athletic pursuits, but ultimately fell short of achieving the rank of general.

But to his credit be it said that in a corrupt time he never used his opportunities for plunder and extortion.
